DICNOS7EUlJM 
TRANSIENTLY 
EXPRESSES EIGHT DISTINCT 
G-PROTEIN a-SUBUNITS DURING ITS DEVELOPMENTAL 
PROGRAM 
Lijun Wu and Peter N. Devreotes’ 
Department 
of Biological 
Chemistry 
The Johns Hopkins 
University 
School of Medicine 
Baltimore, 
MD 21205 
Received 
August 
5, 
1991 
Guanine 
nucleotide-binding 
proteins 
(G-proteins) 
play important 
roles in signal 
transduction 
and in controlling 
the developmental 
program 
of Dic~os~e/~u~ 
discoideum. 
While five G-protein 
a-subunits 
have been identified 
in this organism, 
several lines of 
evidence suggest that additional 
G-proteins 
exist. Using the polymerase 
chain reaction 
technique, 
we have isolated three novel G-protein 
a-subunit genes. The predicted amino 
acid sequences 
of these PCR fragments are very similar to other Dic~ostelium 
G-protein 
a-subunits 
in the conserved 
regions and share little homology 
outside of those regions. 
Northern 
blot analyses 
indicate that each of these genes has very distinct pattern of 
expression 
during development. 
The possible roles of these G-protein 
a-subunits 
are 
discussed. 
0 1991 Academic 
Pres*, Inc. 
When deprived of nutrients, Dictyosfelium 
discoideum 
amoebae 
cease growth and 
initiate a relatively simple and rapid developmental 
program. 
This program consists of the 
aggregation 
of lo5 individual amoebae to form a multicellular 
mound that differentiates into 
a migrating 
slug and eventually 
culminates 
to form a fruiting body. 
This spontaneous 
process is organized 
by extracellular 
adenosine 
3’,5’- monophosphate 
@AMP), which 
binds to surface receptors that mediate chemotaxis 
and oscillatory 
cell-cell signaling 
(1). 
The CAMP receptors contain seven transmembrane 
domains 
(2,3), a characteristic 
of G- 
protein-linked 
receptors. 
G-proteins 
are membrane-associated 
heterotrimers 
composed 
of a, f3, and y 
subunits. 
In most systems, the a subunit determines 
the specificity of the interaction with 
receptors and effector molecules. 
Five G-protein a-subunit genes have been cloned from 
Dictyostelium 
(4-6). All of the genes encode the highly conserved domains 
characteristic 
of a-subunits. 
The time courses of expression of these genes have been characterized 
and some of them have been shown to play important 
roles in signal transduction 
and 
’ To whom correspondence 
should be addressed.

COMMUNICATIONS 
in controlling 
the 
developmental 
program 
(5,7,8, 
Hadwiger 
and 
Firtel, 
personal 
communication). 
Ga2, for example, 
is required for the early aggregation 
process and 
most of the known CAMP receptor-mediated 
responses 
(7,8) and Ga4 plays a role in 
morphogenesis 
of 
the 
multicellular 
structure 
(Hadwiger 
and 
Firtel, 
personal 
communication). 
The functions of the other G-proteins 
are being investigated. 
However, 
several lines of evidence suggest that additional 
G-proteins exist. First, cellular responses 
involved with chemotaxis 
to folic acid are thought to be mediated 
by G-proteins coupling 
with cell surface receptors since folic acid binding is modulated 
by guanine 
nucleotides 
(9-l 2). Secondly, Dicfyostelium 
adenylate cyclase is strongly activated by GTP and GTP- 
y-S (13,14). 
The G-proteins 
identified previously are unlikely to fulfill these roles, since 
neither folic acid chemotaxis 
nor the activation of adenylate cyclase was affected in Gal-, 
Ga2-, 
Ga3-, 
or 
GaC 
disrupted 
cells 
(5,8,15, 
Hadwiger 
and 
Fittel, 
personal 
communication), 
and Ga5 is expressed much later in the development 
(Hadwiger 
and 
Firtel, personal 
communication). 
Therefore, 
we decided to further explore the diversity 
of G-proteins in this organism. 
This report describes the discovery of three additional 
a- 
MATERIALS 
AND METHODS 
Polvmerase 
chain 
reactions 
(PCRs). 
PCR 
was carried 
out 
using 
either 
Dictyosfelium 
AX-3 genomic 
DNA or cDNA generated from RNA isolated from cells 3 hrs 
after nutrient depletion. 
Primers LWl 
and LW4 correspond 
to the sense strand that 
encodes the amino acid sequence G(AT)GESGK 
and D(AV)(GA)GQR 
respectively; 
and 
primers LW2 and LW3 correspond 
to the antisense strand that encodes the amino acid 
sequence TCA(lV)DT 
and D(AV)(GA)GQR 
respectively. 
The sequences 
of the primers 
are: 
LWl 5’CCGGATCCGG(AT)(AG)C(CAT)GG(AT)GA(GA)(AT)(GC)(CAT)GG(AT)AA; 
LW2 5’CCGTCGACGT(AG)TC(ATG)(AG)~C)(GAT)GC(GA)CA(GAT)GT; 
LW3 %CGTCGAC(TG)(TC)TG(TA)CC(GAT)(CG)C(GAT)(GA)C(GA)TC; 
LW4 5’CCGGATCCGA(CT)G(CT)(CTA)G(GC)O-AC)GGCTA)CA(AG)(AC)G. 
The sequence of LWl and LW4 contains a BarnHI restriction site and the sequence 
of LW2 and LW3 contains a SalI restriction site to facilitate subcloning. 
Thirty amplification 
cycles consisting 
of 5 min at 94“C, 1.5 min at 55’C, and 3 min at 72°C were performed, 
followed by a final extension 
step (8 min at 72%). 
The reaction products were purified 
by GeneClean 
(BiolOl, 
La Jolla, CA), digested 
by BamHl 
and S&l, and subcloned 
to 
pBluescript 
KS/- (Stratagene, 
Burlingame, 
CA). 
Southern Hybridization. 
Southern blot hybridization 
and colony hybridization 
were 
carried out as described 
by Maniatis et a/. (16). DNA probes were made by the random 
priming 
method 
(17). 
manalvsis. 
DNA sequencing 
reactions were performed 
using the 
DNA 
Sequenase, 
version 2.0 kit according 
to the instructions 
provided 
by the manufacturer 
(United States Biochemical, 
Cleveland, 
OH). Double strand miniprep 
DNA for use as a 
sequencing 
template 
was prepared 
by the boiling lysis method 
(16). Clones were first 
grouped 
according 
to the pattern obtained 
from a single lane of DNA sequencing, 
and 
the complete 
DNA sequence was determined 
for representatives 
of each pattern.

Northern blot analysis. 
For the development 
time course of Ga gene expression, 
D. discoideum 
strain AX-3 cells grown in HL-5 medium 
were washed and plated on DB 
plates for development 
(18). Total RNA was isolated from cells harvested at specific times 
in development 
by the guanidine-HCI 
procedure 
(19) sized on a denaturing 
gel, and 
analyzed by RNA blot hybridization 
as described 
(16). 
RESULTS 
AND DISCUSSION 
Clonina of PCR fragments 
encodina for Ga6. Ga7. and Ga8 subunits. 
To isolate 
additional 
DicryosteIium 
a-subunit 
genes, 
we used 
short, 
degenerate 
primers 
in 
polymerase 
chain reactions 
on Dictyosfelium 
genomic 
DNA or cDNA. 
The primers 
correspond to the amino acid sequences of the putative GTP-binding 
and GTPase regions 
and other highly conserved 
regions of known a-subunits. 
The PCR fragments 
were 
subcloned, 
and the clones containing 
Gal to Ga5 genes were eliminated 
by hybridization. 
The newly discovered 
a-subunits 
were identified by comparing 
the predicted amino acid 
sequence of each clone to the sequence of the known Ga proteins in the most conserved 
regions. 
Three sets of primers were used in the PCR reactions. 
When primers LWl and 
LW2, or LW2 and LW4 were used, only Gal 
to Ga5 clones and clones containing 
unknown 
sequences 
were obtained. 
These unknown 
sequences 
did not have any 
homology 
to the known Ga genes and they did not contain any major open reading 
frames, thus they are probably 
PCR artifacts. 
When primers LWl and LW3 were used 
in PCR, 
about 
l/3 
of the clones 
analyzed 
fell into three novel a-subunit 
classes 
designated 
as Ga6, Ga7, and Ga8. The others contained unknown sequences and were 
not further studied. 
Ga6 clones were obtained 
only when using genomic 
DNA as a 
template, 
and Ga7 clones were obtained 
only when using cDNA as a template. 
Ga8 
clones, however, were obtained when using either genomic 
DNA or cDNA as a template. 
Genomic 
Southern 
blot analyses 
showed that Ga6, Ga7, and Ga8 PCR fragments 
recognize distinct bands of Dictyostelium 
genomic 
DNA (data not shown). These results 
indicate 
that Ga6, Ga7, and Ga8 clones are amplified 
from contiguous 
segments 
of 
Dh’yosfe/ium 
DNA. 
Seauence 
comparisons 
amona 
Dh3vosfelium 
Ga subunits. 
The nucleotide 
sequences 
of Ga6, Ga7, and Ga8 PCR products were determined 
and a comparison 
of 
the 
predicted 
amino 
acid sequences 
of these 
PCR fragments 
to the previously 
characterized 
Ga subunits of Dicfyostelium 
is shown in figure 1. Ga6, Ga7, and Ga8 are 
25 - 31% identical to each other and are 32 - 41% identical to Gal 
and Ga2. 
These 
overall percentages 
are expected 
to be low since the amplified 
region 
is the least 
conserved 
stretch among all a-subunits.

Figure 1. Comparison 
of the deduced amino acid sequences 
of Ga6, Ga7, and 
Ga8 PCR products to that of the other Dictyostelium G-protein a-subunits. Amino acid 
sequences for Gal and Ga2 are from Pupillo eta/. (4). *: The complete sequence of Ga3 
is shown in Pupillo et a/. (5). + : The cloning and sequencing of Ga4 and Ga5 are shown 
in Hadwiger et al. (6). A short segment of Ga3, Ga4, and Ga5 sequences are shown 
here to distinguish them from other G-protein a-subunits. The sequences of Ga7 and 
Ga8 were obtained from PCR fragments amplified from Dicfyostelium cDNA and the 
sequence of Ga6 was obtained from a PCR fragment amplified from genomic DNA. An 
intron was found in the Ga6 clones and the coding sequence was derived based on the 
consensus exon-intron boundary sequences. The amino acid sequences chosen to 
design the PCR primers are shown on the top with the name and direction of each 
oligonucleotide. The consensus amino acid sequences were compiled from the 
sequences of each G-protein a-subunit shown. Amino acids in boldface are identical to 
the consensus sequences. 
However, 
some amino 
acid residues 
are highly conserved 
among 
all the Ga 
subunits. 
A consensus 
amino acid sequence 
was compiled 
from the most conserved 
positions 
(occurring 
in more than 75% of the cases) in the amplified 
region within the 
primed sites. Each Ga sequence contains more than 82% of the consensus amino acids. 
Outside of these regions, the amino acid sequences show little homology. 
Based on this 
limited sequence 
information, 
the Dictyostelium 
G-proteins 
do not bear a direct relation 
to the a-subunit 
classes, such as Gs, Gi, Gq, etc., found in higher eucatyotes 
(20). Nor 
do the eight u-subunits 
fall into subtypes. 
The 
RNA isolated 
from wild-type Dictyostelium 
(strain AX-3) cells at different stages of development 
was 
probed with Ga6, Ga7, and Ga8 PCR fragments 
(figure 2). Ga6 was expressed primarily 
in vegetative cells. The level of the Ga6 mRNA (about 1.8 kb) declined 
rapidly upon the 
initiation 
of development. 
Three hours after nutrient depletion 
(corresponding 
to very 
early aggregation), only a very weak signal was observed, and by 6 hr no band was

Figure 2. Developmental regulation of Ga6, Ga7, and Go18 expression. 
Total RNA 
isolated from Dicfyostelium 
wild-type 
(AX-3) cells at the times (hr) indicated was 
fractionated on a denaturing gel; blotted onto nitrocellulose filters: and probed with Ga6, 
Ga7, and Ga8 PCR fragments. 
Each lane contained 6 pg RNA. 
“0” indicates RNA 
isolated from vegetative cells. During this developmental time course, visible aggregates 
were observed at 5-6 hr, mounds were formed at 9-10 hr, and fruiting bodies were found 
at 24 hr. Numbers on the right indicate the size of the RNAs in kb. 
detected. 
Ga7 hybridized 
to two mRNA species 
(2.0 kb and 2.5 kb respectively) 
at 
different stages of development. 
A minor 2.0-kb band was detected in growing cells and 
was absent in starved cells. 
The level of the major Ga7 RNA (2.5 kb) peaked 
in late 
aggregation 
and early mound 
stages (6 to 9 hrs), and declined 
thereafter. 
Ga8 also 
hybridized 
to two mRNAs (1.9 kb and 2.3 kb respectively). 
The 2.3 kb species was not 
expressed 
or expressed 
at very low levels in vegetative 
cells. 
After the initiation 
of 
development, 
levels of this RNA species were induced 
and both species reached 
a 
maximal 
level during aggregation 
(between 3 and 6 hrs). The levels of the Ga8 RNAs 
declined 
in the late development. 
The distinct expression 
pattern of Gcc~, Ga7, and Ga8 genes suggest that these 
G proteins may be involved in different signal transduction 
pathways. 
Ga6 is expressed 
primarily 
in vegetative 
cells and thus it might be involved 
in the folic acid chemotaxis 
signal transduction 
pathway. The time course of Ga8 correlates with that of the adenylate 
cyclase activity in Dictyosfelium 
(21,22) and is a potential 
analogue 
for the Gs-like G- 
proteins which stimulate adenylate cyclase in mammals. 
To further study these novel Ga 
genes, experiments 
are in progress to screen for genomic and cDNA clones, to determine 
the complete 
nucleotide 
sequences, 
and to make gene-disruption 
mutants to investigate 
their functions.

BIOCHEMICAL 
AND BIOPHYSICAL 
RESEARCH COMMUNICATIONS 
In conclusion, 
the discovery of three additional Ga genes in Dicfyosfelium 
suggests 
that this primitive 
multicellular 
microorganism 
expresses 
at least eight 
G-protein 
a- 
subunits. 
More novel a-subunits 
might 
exist whose sequence 
is divergent 
in the 
conserved 
regions such that our PCR techniques 
would not have detected 
them. 
In 
mammalian 
system, there are 17 Ga subunits identified 
(20). The remarkable 
degree of 
similarity 
in the mechanisms 
of signal transduction 
among 
Dictyostelium 
and higher 
eukaryotes 
makes this biochemically 
and genetically 
accessible 
organism 
an attractive 
model system to study G-protein function. 
ACKNOWLEDGMENTS

for their communication 
of results before publication. 
We also would like to thank all the 
members 
of the Devreotes’s

reaction, J. Milne for reading this manuscript, 
and D. Hereld for help with the preperation 
of figure 2. This work was supported 
by 
NIH grant GM28007 
to P.N.D. 
NOTE 
PCR fragments 
containing 
sequences 
very similar 
to that of the Ga6 clones 
described 
here were also obtained 
independently 
in another 
lab (R. Firtel, personal 
communication). 
The two clones were different for three amino acid residues.
